body.text: Design language for the generated website. Typography (font family, sizes, weights), color palette (accent, background, text), spacing scale, layout grid, border / radius / shadow conventions. Codegen reads this on every regen so the chrome and per-cell deps converge on one aesthetic. Edit freely; new instructions take effect on the next /website upgrade.
allow a light and a dark theme
icons to the left of headings so we have some nice organization

Theme tokens are CSS custom properties on :root for the dark default, plus a [data-theme='light'] override block for the light theme. Every chrome rule reads colors / surfaces / borders / shadows through these vars so a single attribute switch flips the whole site.

Theme persistence: store the user's choice in localStorage under a site-scoped key (e.g. 'ibh:theme'). On first paint, an inline script in <head> reads the key and stamps data-theme on <html> before stylesheet apply, avoiding flash. Fallback when no key is set: a @media (prefers-color-scheme: light) :root:not([data-theme]) block mirrors the light overrides — so OS preference wins until the user picks.

Heading-icon shape: every heading splits into a __title-icon span (leading) and a __title-text span. The wrapper is flex with align-items:center and a small gap. Icons are inline SVG sized via the heading's font-size (1em square) so they scale with type. Stroke-only line icons keep the visual weight light next to large display type.

Voice and tone the generated copy should adopt. Formal vs casual, terse vs expansive, technical vs everyday vocabulary. Audience expectations. Voice rules become hard constraints in codegen — say what you mean here so the site sounds like you.

Technology constraints for codegen. Framework choice (vanilla HTML/CSS, vue, react, svelte). Browser targets, performance budgets, accessibility level (WCAG AA?), bundle size limits. Set hard rules here so codegen does not invent dependencies you do not want.

The reader of the generated site — their context, what they already know, what they are trying to learn or accomplish. Codegen calibrates depth, jargon, and section pacing to this audience description.

Reference exemplars — sites whose style or structure should inspire the generation. Drop signatures of resources, links to real sites, or notes describing patterns to emulate. Codegen ingests these as positive examples.

Always-on context for every codegen request. Sub-cells under here divide concerns — styles, voice, tech, audience, examples. Edit any sub-cell to refine what Claude considers when generating chrome and per-cell deps. Never rendered as website pages; always read as the prompt envelope. Codegen also writes its own design decisions back here so the implicit choices stay visible to you.
